evals.name: monorepo-trigger-explosion
evals.prompt:
we just split our company monorepo into 5 different concourse pipelines (one per service). problem: every commit to main now triggers ALL 5 pipelines, even if the commit only touched one service. we have ~20 commits a day, so workers are constantly busy rebuilding stuff that didn't actually change. our git resource for one of the services looks like this:

```yaml
resources:
  - name: src
    type: git
    source:
      uri: https://github.com/example/monorepo.git
      branch: main
```

the service we care about for THIS pipeline lives in `services/orders/` and shares utility code in `libs/shared/`. tests inside the service include a `tests/` subdirectory but markdown files (READMEs, ADRs etc) shouldn't trigger anything. how do i scope this so it only fires on relevant changes? please show the corrected resource yaml + brief explanation.
evals.expected_output: Resource YAML with `paths: [services/orders/**, libs/shared/**]` and `ignore_paths: ["**/*.md"]` (or similar). Brief explanation of `paths` (allow-list) vs `ignore_paths` (deny-list) and the order of evaluation. May also mention `webhook_token` as a follow-up for cutting check load.

assertions.name: uses paths filter
assertions.description: Output YAML includes a `paths:` list that scopes to the relevant directories (services/orders, libs/shared)

assertions.name: uses ignore_paths for markdown
assertions.description: Output YAML includes an `ignore_paths:` entry that excludes `.md` files

assertions.name: preserves git source basics
assertions.description: Output keeps `uri`, `branch: main`, and the `git` type

assertions.name: explains paths vs ignore_paths
assertions.description: Response explains that `paths` is an allow-list and `ignore_paths` is applied after, or otherwise clarifies their interaction

assertions.name: valid yaml
assertions.description: Output YAML is syntactically valid Concourse v8 syntax

evals.name: registry-image-silent-breakage
evals.prompt:
yesterday's deploys started failing in prod because our base image got rebuilt with a breaking change upstream. we have:

```yaml
resources:
  - name: golang-builder
    type: registry-image
    source:
      repository: golang
      tag: latest
      username: ((dockerhub.username))
      password: ((dockerhub.password))
```

the team wants reproducibility but we ALSO want to pick up patch releases automatically (security fixes etc). how do we get the best of both? show what the resource should look like and explain the tradeoff between digest pinning vs semver-constraining. assume our app needs go 1.22.x and we'd like to auto-pick patch versions but NOT auto-jump to 1.23.
evals.expected_output: Resource YAML using `semver_constraint: "~1.22"` (or `">=1.22 <1.23"`) instead of `tag: latest`. May also mention digest pin as an alternative for full reproducibility. Explanation of the tradeoff: digest = full reproducibility but no auto-updates; semver_constraint = auto patches but trust upstream.

assertions.name: removes tag latest
assertions.description: Output YAML does NOT use `tag: latest`

assertions.name: uses semver_constraint
assertions.description: Output YAML includes a `semver_constraint:` that pins to 1.22.x (e.g. "~1.22", ">=1.22 <1.23", or "1.22.x")

assertions.name: explains digest alternative
assertions.description: Response mentions digest pinning as an alternative for full reproducibility

assertions.name: explains tradeoff
assertions.description: Response explains the tradeoff between digest (no auto-updates) vs semver_constraint (auto patches)

assertions.name: valid yaml
assertions.description: Output YAML is syntactically valid Concourse v8 syntax

evals.name: hammering-github-checks
evals.prompt:
github support sent us a friendly note that we're hitting their API rate limits with our concourse cluster. we have 18 pipelines that each track a `latest-release` from the same github repo (different consumers of the same library), and they all check at the default ~1m interval. that's 18 pipelines × 60 checks/hour = 1080 check requests/hour for the same data, which is silly. how do we cut that down without losing 'fire when there's a new release' behavior?

relevant resource snippet (same in all 18 pipelines):

```yaml
  - name: latest-release
    type: github-release
    source:
      owner: example
      repository: shared-lib
      access_token: ((github_token))
```

what options do i have, and which would you actually pick? assume we run our own concourse cluster and have admin access.
evals.expected_output: Multiple options ranked: (1) raise `check_every` to 10m or 30m on each resource (cheap, immediate), (2) configure `webhook_token` and point GitHub webhooks at the resource (push instead of poll — best latency), (3) operator-side: enable global resources (`CONCOURSE_ENABLE_GLOBAL_RESOURCES`) so the same source config dedupes to one check across all pipelines. Mention security caveat for global resources (credentials de-facto shared).

assertions.name: recommends check_every increase
assertions.description: Response recommends raising `check_every` to a longer interval (e.g. 10m, 30m, 1h)

assertions.name: recommends webhook_token
assertions.description: Response mentions `webhook_token` and the webhook setup as a way to flip from poll to push

assertions.name: mentions global resources
assertions.description: Response mentions enabling global resources (CONCOURSE_ENABLE_GLOBAL_RESOURCES or `--enable-global-resources`) as an operator-level fix

assertions.name: explains global resources tradeoff
assertions.description: Response explains the security/credentials caveat of global resources (shared check load + de-facto credential sharing across teams)

assertions.name: shows updated yaml
assertions.description: Response includes a corrected resource YAML showing at least one of: `check_every:` set to a longer interval, `webhook_token:` added

assertions.name: valid yaml
assertions.description: Output YAML is syntactically valid Concourse v8 syntax
